content: GLEIF Launches vLEIs Using KERI and ACDC

author: No author specified
content: The Global Legal Entity Identifier Foundation (GLEIF) is now using open-source KERI and ACDC technologies to power verifiable Legal Entity Identifiers (vLEIs). These digital credentials let companies prove their identity in a secure and automated way, helping reduce fraud in business communications like calls and messages.

author: No author specified
content: This global rollout is a major step toward trusted digital identity for organisations, starting with use cases in telecom and expanding to other industries.

At the heart of the autonomous identity revolution  Key Event Receipt Infrastructure (KERI) is the first truly fully decentralized identity system. Truly Decentralized Identity

KERI is the first truly decentralized identity system. KERI identifiers are truly portable, as there is no ledger lock-in, there is no central authority, and no compromise on privacy or security. It uses self-certifying identifiers and cryptographic key events to enable secure, portable, and verifiable identities across the internet. Think of it as the foundation for a trust-first digital world.

KERI is inherently supportive of GDPR (global data protection rights) compliance. KERI provides non-intertwined identifier trust bases which means that a given identifier’s data may be erased and truly forgotten. ow available!

KERI has a decentralized secure root-of-trust based on cryptographic self-certifying identifiers, so trust doesn’t depend on any central authority. It uses hash chained data structures called Key Event Logs that enable ambient cryptographic verifiability. In other words, any log may be verified anywhere at anytime by anybody. It has separable control over shared data which gives each person or entity real control over their own identifiers and shared data.  Learn more about KERI…

author: No author specified
content: The KERI Suite

author: No author specified
content: The KERI Suite aims to make KERI easier to adopt and implement. It is a growing set of open-source tools and protocols that makes secure, decentralized digital identity possible. It allows individuals, organizations, and devices to prove who they are, control their own identifiers, and verify data authenticity across the internet, all while preserving privacy and autonomy.

author: No author specified
content: The KERI Suite offers a resilient foundation for trust in critical sectors like finance, healthcare, IoT, and public services. The KERI Suite focuses on reducing complexity, so that anyone can deploy decentralized identity solutions with minimal effort and technical knowledge, across platforms and use cases.

author: No author specified
content: The foundation’s long-term objective is to establish a not-for-profit organization that generates funds. This organization will ensure the security and reputation of the KERI Suite while supporting its user base and donors with open-source code, various services, and transparent business relationships with companies.

author: No author specified
content: With financial resources secured and the foundation’s structure established, we can focus on maturing the tooling (proof of concept). Allocating funds takes precedence over all other considerations, such as supporting the open-source base. Our primary goal is to reduce complications by addressing the 5 W’s: Wallet, Witness, Watcher, Web, and Wizard.

author: No author specified
content: Karla McKenna is an international standards specialist in financial services. Ms. McKenna is Managing Director, GLEIF Americas, as well as Head of Standards for the Global Legal Entity Identifier Foundation. Ms. McKenna led the development of the digital use of the LEI, concentrating on the implementation of the verifiable LEI (vLEI), and is also responsible for facilitating the development and implementation of GLEIF standards and leveraging international standards to maximize data quality and the operational integrity of the Global LEI System.

author: No author specified
content: Ms. McKenna chaired the International Organization for Standardization Technical Committee 68 (ISO/TC 68), Financial services, from 2006 to 2018, and continues ISO work in the areas of standards interoperability and regulatory use of international standards as co-convenor of the TC 68 Standards Advisory Group.

author: No author specified
content: Ms. McKenna is a member of the inaugural Boards of the Eurofiling Foundation and the KERI Foundation, the Steering Committee of Trust over IP, and the transitional Board of the Ayra Foundation; is the Immediate Past Chair of the Securities Market Practice Group (SMPG) and also has served on the Boards of XBRL International, the International Securities Association for Institutional Trade Communication (ISITC) and the Accredited Standards Committee X9 (ASC X9).

content: Identity and Security

author: No author specified
content: The problem with Internet security is that it’s based on flawed identity, and security experts don’t understand identity well enough to fix it.

author: No author specified
content: The problem with Internet identity is that it’s based on flawed security, and identity experts don’t understand security well enough to fix it.

author: No author specified
content: KERI’s design reflects a sufficient understanding of both security and identity to fix both Internet identity and Internet security at the same time.

author: No author specified
content: ~ Samuel Smith, August 2024

author: No author specified
content: Lead Role for the inventor

author: No author specified
content: Like a few successful examples of open-source software foundations, we have organized that KERI inventor Sam Smith can rule over the KERI Foundation. This will change over the coming years. But we start by putting Dr. Smith in the driver’s seat.

author: No author specified
content: Once the ethos around the KERI Suite has matured, his role could be more relaxed and less stringent.

author: No author specified
content: Coopetive design

author: No author specified
content: Sam has always been a builder of systems at scale. Nothing is better than a real-life proof of your design, concepts, and standardizations.This is why Sam has also been focussing on infrastructure for KERI. He saw that such infrastructure did not exist for secure identifier systems at scale.

author: No author specified
content: Compatible solution for business-minded actors in the foundation

author: No author specified
content: To look for economic incentives to make web-scale infrastructure work for the new ideas he has (had), like the KERI Suite, is something Dr. Smith will continue to do. The foundation’s design is coopetive: the time to put in a business initiative has to healthily compete with the time put into taking up roles and executing tasks of the foundation. In other words, the foundation strives to pay people market-conform.
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Compatible solution for business-minded actors in the foundation

author: No author specified
content: The foundation will run as a role-based business, and these coopetive characteristics apply to every manager and actor in the foundation.

content: Advantages of L3C over LLC

author: No author specified
content: private non-profit foundations can invest in an L3C without losing their non-profit status.

author: No author specified
content: L3C can emphasize its mission over making a profit. That implies that not making a profit is perfectly fine and does not lead to situations where somebody can be sued for decisions not to make a profit.

author: No author specified
content: Positioning in the marketplace

author: No author specified
content: We should position this open-source not-for-profit vehicle alongside and in cooperation with commercial entities and governmental/international umbrella organizations.It has to be clear to all involved that the money you donate to a foundation may have inevitable spin-offs but, in general, does not have financial leverage nor gives donors ultimate power over the foundation. Conversely, commercial ventures can have all this. We can set up the foundation to achieve good harmony with future participating companies—any company, even backed by VCs.

author: No author specified
content: We could design a way to motivate commercial projects to donate a share of their invoices to the foundation (principle 4).

author: No author specified
content: Open-source development and maintenance of the KERI Suite

author: No author specified
content: Development is targeted at maturing the KERI Suite and maintenance on availability and security.
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Open-source development and maintenance of the KERI Suite

author: No author specified
content: Reduce complication

author: No author specified
content: by: Sam Smith

author: No author specified
content: In the following order, we reduce the complication that the newly invented KERI Suite introduced, along five consecutive W’s:

author: No author specified
content: Wallet – inception and further key management

author: No author specified
content: Witness – promulgation of key state

author: No author specified
content: Watchers – validation of authoritative key state

author: No author specified
content: Web – infrastructure -> 3W leverage it -> API

author: No author specified
content: Wizard – Guiderails & bumpers -> 10 page manual

author: No author specified
content: Developers who examine the current complications of KERI ask themselves: Can I swiftly get a prototype up and running?

author: No author specified
content: The construction of a community Minimum Viable Product that the foundation offers as open source (Apache2).

author: No author specified
content: Availability and security

author: No author specified
content: Improve and maintain the open source code base. (Find funds to) Manage the open source development of the KERI Suite based on its standards documents and keep the code base secure and available.Security is the main selling point of the KERI Suite. The foundation is the image of this. The chain of all implementations together is as strong as the weakest link.That’s why we certify all code offered.

author: No author specified
content: Service to donors

author: No author specified
content: Directed funding

author: No author specified
content: Donors are free to designate and even define projects within the foundation where the main share of their money will go to. We call this service direct funding. There are several prerequisites for projects to be directly funded.

author: No author specified
content: The reason why we have a few rules is that we can’t have the reputation of the KERI Suite or the foundation put on the line because some group wants to code whether they like. They can, of course, but not from inside the foundation.⁠If you want more freedom, you can, Sam/the responsible team/foundation may or may not certify your code. If you get off track with your code (insecure, a fork, or something like that), then the foundation will label your working group as a company, not related to the foundation.

author: No author specified
content: Tax-exempt and tax-deductibility

author: No author specified
content: Tax-exempt means an entity or natural person doesn’t have to pay taxes in their jurisdiction, or you’re a citizen over the gift you give to a charity with a particular domicile. We refer to a tax deduction when a possible donor could deduct a gift to a charity from his taxable income.

author: No author specified
content: As a foundation, we should offer as many tax exemptions as possible to donors from prominent donor countries, even if that needs setting up a subsidiary of the foundation in those countries.

author: No author specified
content: Tax deductibility is too hard for a foundation to achieve because of the variation in individual cases. However, we will offer general guidelines and advice to consult a tax specialist.

author: No author specified
content: Benefits for donors to the foundation

author: No author specified
content: The Foundation offers four levels of donorship: Strategic, Contributing, Associate, and Committer.

author: No author specified
content: Sponsor the KERI Foundation

author: No author specified
content: The KERI Foundation welcomes sponsors who want to help shape the future of secure, decentralized identity. While members contribute primarily time and expertise, sponsors provide financial support. Both are essential to building a trusted system!

author: No author specified
content: Choose a way to contribute that fits you best:

author: No author specified
content: General SponsorshipSupport the Foundation’s core mission with flexible funding that powers research, infrastructure, and community growth.

author: No author specified
content: Project-Based FundingFinance and execute your own KERI-related project, either solo or in collaboration with others. Always with guidance and oversight from the KERI Foundation to ensure alignment with our goals.

author: No author specified
content: Sponsorship of the Open Source CodebaseFund development directly by sponsoring contributors to improve KERI.

author: No author specified
content: Transparency, fairness, and long-term trust are at the core of every partnership. That’s why we clearly outline our sponsorship principles, to ensure shared expectations from the start.

author: No author specified
content: No pay-to-playFinancial support does not buy control, but it does come with visibility and influence.

author: No author specified
content: Mission-firstThe Foundation stays neutral, inclusive, and independent, always putting the integrity of the system first.

author: No author specified
imgMeta:
KERI implementations Here is an overview of the KERI implementations we are currently aware of. Finema  Finema Contact: Nuttawut Kongsuwan

Where: Thailand

Description: At Finema (Thailand), we have R&D and pilot projects using KERI.  GLEIF GLEIF

Contact: Alexandre Kech

Where: Switzerland

Description: We are at GLEIF with the vLEI and have incorporated it into the ISO 17442 LEI standard.  RootsID passion project is DoulaID, led by Annie Byrd, which extends the vLEI credential chain and EGF to improve Maternal Mortality rates in the US. We are also leveraging the KERI protocols to implement cross-border, cross-platform, cross-chain identity into the Cardano blockchain ecosystem, along with Cardano Foundation Sebastian Bode Thomas A. Mayfield Fergal O’Connor Jaime Caso Onzain and Cardano community.

content: Come work for the KERI Foundation!

author: No author specified
content: The KERI Foundation is currently seeking the following developer profiles:
firstHeadingBeforeElement: Come work for the KERI Foundation!

author: No author specified
content: Organisation

author: No author specified
content: Our team is contributing to the future of the KERI Suite of protocols as the trust spanning layer for the internet. This future is based upon open standards. These include Trust over IP (ToIP) Foundation’s, KERI, ACDC, and CESR specifications. ToIP resides inside the Linux Foundation’s Decentralized Trust umbrella. Our main objective is to build what we call “1-click KERI”. This would enable any prospective adopter to download and deploy a functional infrastructure. The five main components we call the five Ws: wallets, witnesses, watchers, web, and wizards. You can learn more about the KERI Suite of protocols here.

author: No author specified
content: Everything we build will be open source (Apache2). We use Python as the core language for backend service development, and micro-web application frameworks for front-end APIs. We leverage open source cryptographic libraries. The architecture takes a distributed, performance-driven, horizontally scalable, asynchronous (coroutine) event-sourcing approach.

author: No author specified
content: About the Role

author: No author specified
content: The full-stack developer will build both backend and frontend functionality for infrastructure components supporting the KERI Suite of protocols. This includes backend APIs that support frontend applications, as well as developing both user and admin frontend applications. We are looking for engineers with solid analytical, research, and programming skills. Production experience in Python and JavaScript programming is a must. Experience with HTMX is preferred. Experience with Pyodide is preferred. Experience in C or Rust is a bonus. Experience or training in distributed communication protocols, decentralized identity, cryptography, and Web3 systems is preferred.

author: No author specified
content: The KERI Foundation’s Intellectual Property Policy is designed to protect the rights of contributors while ensuring that the community benefits from open, accessible, and innovative technologies. By adhering to this policy, we can foster a collaborative environment that advances the development of decentralized identity technologies for the benefit of all.

author: No author specified
content: Introduction

author: No author specified
content: The KERI Foundation is committed to fostering innovation and collaboration in the development of a decentralized infrastructure for identifier technologies. This Intellectual Property Policy (IPP) is designed to protect the rights of all contributors while ensuring that the community benefits from open and accessible code, specifications, and documentation.Our goal is to create a transparent, fair, and inclusive environment that encourages contribution and participation.

author: No author specified
content: 1. Purpose

author: No author specified
content: This policy outlines the principles governing the ownership, licensing, and use of intellectual property (IP) within the KERI Foundation. It aims to:

author: No author specified
content: Encourage and protect open-source contributions.

author: No author specified
content: Ensure that all contributions are appropriately licensed.

author: No author specified
content: Clarify the rights and responsibilities of contributors, maintainers, and users.

author: No author specified
content: 2. Types of Intellectual Property

author: No author specified
content: The KERI Foundation recognizes and protects the following types of intellectual property:

author: No author specified
content: Copyright: Protection for original works of authorship, including software code, documentation, and other content.

author: No author specified
content: Trademarks: Protection for brand names, logos, and other identifiers associated with the KERI Foundation and its projects.

author: No author specified
content: Patents: Protection for inventions, including methods, processes, and technologies that may be developed within the scope of the KERI Foundation’s activities.

author: No author specified
content: Code: Protection for software code, including ownership, licensing, and contribution rights to ensure security, innovation, and alignment with open standards.

author: No author specified
content: 3. Contribution and Licensing

author: No author specified
content: 3.1. Contributor License Agreement (CLA)

author: No author specified
content: All contributors must sign a Contributor License Agreement (CLA) before their contributions can be accepted into any KERI Foundation project. The CLA ensures that contributors grant the necessary rights to their contributions while retaining their ownership.

author: No author specified
content: 3.2. Licensing of Contributions

author: No author specified
content: Open Source Licensing: All software contributions to KERI Foundation projects must be made under the Apache License 2.0. This license ensures that the software remains open and accessible while providing contributors with clear terms regarding their rights and responsibilities.

author: No author specified
content: Documentation and Other Contributions: Non-software contributions, such as documentation, should be licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0 International License (CC BY-SA 4.0) or a similar open content license.

author: No author specified
content: 4. Trademarks

author: No author specified
content: Use of the KERI Foundation’s trademarks must be approved by the Foundation and must comply with the Foundation’s trademark usage guidelines. Unauthorized use of the Foundation’s trademarks is strictly prohibited.

author: No author specified
content: 5. Patents

author: No author specified
content: 5.1. Patent Non-Assertion

author: No author specified
content: The KERI Foundation encourages a patent non-assertion policy. Contributors and participants are encouraged not to assert patent claims against KERI Foundation projects and users for the use, sale, or distribution of software developed by the Foundation.

author: No author specified
content: 5.2. Patent Grants

author: No author specified
content: If a contributor has or later acquires patent rights that may be infringed by the use or implementation of their contribution, the contributor agrees to grant a non-exclusive, royalty-free, worldwide, irrevocable license to such patent rights to the KERI Foundation and its users.

author: No author specified
content: 6. Ownership and Attribution

author: No author specified
content: Ownership: Contributors retain ownership of their contributions but must grant the KERI Foundation the necessary rights to use, modify, and distribute those contributions under the terms of the applicable license.

author: No author specified
content: Attribution: The KERI Foundation values and respects the work of its contributors. All contributions will be attributed to the original author(s) in a manner consistent with the project’s practices.

author: No author specified
content: 7. Third-Party Contributions

author: No author specified
content: Contributions that include third-party intellectual property must be clearly identified and must have the appropriate permissions and licenses in place. Contributors are responsible for ensuring that their contributions do not infringe on others’ IP rights.

author: No author specified
content: 8. Dispute Resolution

author: No author specified
content: The KERI Foundation is committed to resolving intellectual property disputes in a fair and transparent manner. Any disputes related to intellectual property will be handled by the Foundation’s legal team and, if necessary, escalated to mediation or arbitration.

author: No author specified
content: 9. Compliance

author: No author specified
content: All participants in KERI Foundation projects must comply with this Intellectual Property Policy. Violations of this policy may result in the rejection of contributions, removal from the project, or other actions deemed necessary by the Foundation.

author: No author specified
content: 10. Amendments

author: No author specified
content: The KERI Foundation reserves the right to amend this Intellectual Property Policy as needed to address evolving legal, technological, and community considerations. Any changes to this policy will be communicated to the community in a timely manner.

author: No author specified
content: These guidelines integrate the collaborative spirit of the Linux Foundation, the inclusivity and respect emphasized by the Python Foundation, and the focus on transparency and mentorship from the Eclipse Foundation. They aim to create a balanced and effective framework for participation in the KERI Foundation.

author: No author specified
content: Introduction

author: No author specified
content: The KERI Foundation is a collaborative, open, and inclusive community dedicated to advancing KERI version of Decentralized Technologies. We believe that our community thrives when everyone participates actively and respectfully. These guidelines are designed to help ensure a positive experience for all participants and to maintain the high standards of behavior and contribution expected in the KERI community.

author: No author specified
content: 1. General Expectations

author: No author specified
content: Respect and Professionalism: Treat all participants with respect and professionalism. Disagreements should be handled constructively, with a focus on resolving issues rather than escalating conflicts.

author: No author specified
content: Inclusivity: Strive to include a diverse range of voices and perspectives in all discussions and decision-making processes. Make an effort to accommodate different communication styles and needs.

author: No author specified
content: Openness: Be open to new ideas, perspectives, and feedback. Engage in transparent communication and decision-making processes.

author: No author specified
content: 2. Contribution Guidelines

author: No author specified
content: High-Quality Contributions: Ensure that your contributions are well-researched, thoughtfully presented, and aligned with the goals and standards of the KERI Foundation. This includes code contributions, documentation, event organization, and community support.

author: No author specified
content: Attribution and Credit: Always give proper credit to the original authors of ideas, code, or documentation. Acknowledge the contributions of others when building upon their work.

author: No author specified
content: Collaboration: Work collaboratively with others, seeking consensus and valuing the input of all contributors. Respect the processes and workflows established by the community.

author: No author specified
content: 3. Communication Guidelines

author: No author specified
content: Clarity and Precision: Communicate clearly and concisely, especially in technical discussions. Ensure that your messages are easy to understand and free from ambiguity.

author: No author specified
content: Constructive Feedback: When providing feedback, focus on constructive criticism that is specific, actionable, and aimed at helping others improve. Avoid personal attacks or dismissive comments.

author: No author specified
content: Active Listening: Listen actively and attentively to others. Acknowledge different viewpoints and respond thoughtfully.

author: No author specified
content: 4. Engagement in Community Activities

author: No author specified
content: Participation: Actively participate in community activities, including meetings, discussions, and events. Your engagement helps to shape the direction and success of the KERI Foundation.

author: No author specified
content: Mentorship: Support and mentor new and less experienced members of the community. Share your knowledge and experience generously to help others grow and succeed.

author: No author specified
content: Responsibility: Take responsibility for your actions and contributions. If you commit to a task or project, follow through and communicate clearly if you encounter challenges.

author: No author specified
content: 5. Reporting Issues and Seeking Help

author: No author specified
content: Safe Environment: If you witness or experience behavior that is inconsistent with these guidelines or the KERI Foundation Code of Conduct, report it promptly to the appropriate channels. The KERI Foundation is committed to addressing all concerns with discretion and care.

author: No author specified
content: Seek Help When Needed: Don’t hesitate to seek help or clarification from community leaders or peers if you are unsure about how to proceed with a contribution or how to navigate community interactions.

author: No author specified
content: 6. Decision-Making Process

author: No author specified
content: Consensus-Driven: Decisions in the KERI Foundation are generally made through consensus. Engage in discussions and contribute your perspective, but also be willing to compromise and support the collective decision.

author: No author specified
content: Transparency: All decisions, especially those affecting the broader community, should be made transparently. Document and share the rationale behind decisions to ensure clarity and understanding.

author: No author specified
content: Respecting Authority: While consensus is the goal, certain decisions may be made by designated leaders or working groups. Respect the roles and responsibilities of these individuals and groups.

author: No author specified
content: Responsibility and Deliverables: Board members have fine-grained responsibilities and are liable. They also strive to meet deliverables specified by the foundation’s objectives and operational goals. Some decisions are made by the board.

author: No author specified
content: How decisions are made when consensus can’t be met is anticipated on and eloborated on in informal and formal documents, like design principles of the KERI Foundation and concept statutes. Despite the contours in our design with respect to says and votes are clear (see Roles and organization), the details have to be filled in: Will there be a vote, and who is eligible to vote, or is the decision made by the board or working group chair, etc. In the event of no consensus, what is the path forward. This may be something the initial board may need to discuss and decided how to approach should this event happen, that hopefully will never come.

author: No author specified
content: 7. Continuous Improvement

author: No author specified
content: Feedback Loops: The KERI Foundation values continuous improvement. Provide feedback on processes, guidelines, and community practices to help us evolve and improve over time.The way to do provide feedback is joining one or both of the KERI-Foundation Meetings; the bi-weekly setup meeting for members in a private github environment (ask to be let with a github username, via the contact form), or the alternating bi-weekly Market & Community Meeting. Of course it’s also possible to leave comments on this site and/or in github issues of the KERI Foundation github site.

author: No author specified
content: Learning and Development: Engage in continuous learning to improve your skills and knowledge in decentralized identity technologies and related fields. Share what you learn with the community.

author: No author specified
content: Conclusion

author: No author specified
content: The success of the KERI Foundation depends on the active and respectful participation of all members. By adhering to these guidelines, we can create a vibrant, inclusive, and productive community that advances the field of decentralized identity for the benefit of all.

author: No author specified
content: This Code of Conduct integrates principles from the Linux Foundation’s focus on collaboration and transparency, the Python Software Foundation’s emphasis on respect and inclusivity, and the Eclipse Foundation’s commitment to diversity and community support.

author: No author specified
content: Introduction

author: No author specified
content: The KERI Foundation is committed to fostering an open and welcoming environment for all community participants. We value diversity, inclusivity, and respect for all individuals and believe that a respectful and collaborative community is essential to achieving our mission of advancing decentralized identity technologies. This Code of Conduct outlines our expectations for participant behavior and the steps for reporting and addressing unacceptable behavior.

author: No author specified
content: 1. Be Respectful and Inclusive

author: No author specified
content: Treat everyone with respect, professionalism, and kindness. Be considerate of differing perspectives and experiences.

author: No author specified
content: Respect cultural differences and be mindful of language and tone.

author: No author specified
content: Value the contributions of every community member and make efforts to create an inclusive environment where all voices can be heard.

author: No author specified
content: 2. Be Open and Collaborative

author: No author specified
content: Embrace transparency in communication and decision-making. Share knowledge, ideas, and experiences openly.

author: No author specified
content: Engage in collaborative problem-solving and strive to contribute to the collective success of the KERI community.

author: No author specified
content: Acknowledge and credit the work and ideas of others.

author: No author specified
content: 3. Foster a Positive Environment

author: No author specified
content: Focus on constructive criticism and providing feedback that is helpful and supportive.

author: No author specified
content: Celebrate successes and learn from mistakes without assigning blame.

author: No author specified
content: Actively contribute to an environment where everyone feels comfortable and valued.

author: No author specified
content: 4. Promote Diversity and Inclusion

author: No author specified
content: Encourage and support the participation of people from diverse backgrounds in all aspects of the KERI community.

author: No author specified
content: Avoid discriminatory behavior, language, and content, including but not limited to discrimination based on race, gender, age, sexual orientation, disability, religion, or nationality.

author: No author specified
content: Promote equal opportunities for learning, collaboration, and leadership within the community.

author: No author specified
content: 5. Communicate with Integrity

author: No author specified
content: Communicate with honesty and integrity. Avoid spreading misinformation or engaging in deceptive practices.

author: No author specified
content: Be clear and transparent about your intentions and affiliations when participating in community discussions or projects.

author: No author specified
content: Honor confidentiality and privacy in all interactions, respecting the rights of others to control their personal information.

author: No author specified
content: 6. Respect Community Guidelines and Policies

author: No author specified
content: Adhere to all community guidelines, policies, and legal requirements when participating in KERI activities.

author: No author specified
content: Respect the intellectual property rights of others and ensure that your contributions are original or properly attributed.

author: No author specified
content: Follow the community’s rules for reporting and addressing issues, including this Code of Conduct.

author: No author specified
content: 7. Reporting Unacceptable Behavior

author: No author specified
content: If you experience or witness behavior that violates this Code of Conduct, report it to the KERI Foundation’s designated contacts or through the official reporting mechanisms. For the current officers, see our website.

author: No author specified
content: All reports will be handled with discretion and confidentiality. The KERI Foundation will take appropriate action in response to any violations, including warnings, suspension, or expulsion from the community.

author: No author specified
content: 8. Enforcement and Accountability

author: No author specified
content: The KERI Foundation commits to enforcing this Code of Conduct consistently and fairly.

author: No author specified
content: Violations of the Code of Conduct will be investigated promptly, and corrective actions will be taken as necessary.

author: No author specified
content: The community is expected to cooperate with any investigations and respect the decisions made by the Foundation.

author: No author specified
content: Reporting contacts

author: No author specified
content: Reporting contacts and officers:

author: No author specified
content: ms. J. Koopman, e-mail jkoopman@keri.foundation

author: No author specified
content: mr. H. van Cann, e-mail hvancann@keri.foundation (co-founder KERI Foundation)

author: No author specified
content: Conclusion

author: No author specified
content: The KERI Foundation is dedicated to maintaining a welcoming, inclusive, and respectful community for everyone involved. By upholding this Code of Conduct, we can work together to create a positive and productive environment supporting decentralized identity technologies’ growth and success.

author: No author specified
content: Archived			 (17)

author: No author specified
content: How can anyone want to participate in this?

author: No author specified
content: Suppose we set this up in a reasonable, safe, and logical way to mature the KERISuite first on a small technical team. And we only then start diversifying; do you see yourself in any role at any point in time in the future? We welcome your positive contributions.

author: No author specified
content: How can I see this differently than a ‘Sam Foundation’?

author: No author specified
content: The foundation is co-founded by and tailored to its BDFL, Samuel Smith. We do this not because we’re his fans but because we understand that foundations that are disconnected from their original visionaries and gatekeepers in the past were less successful than the ones that managed to keep them happy and on board. Ideally, a BDFL is a two-way commitment.

author: No author specified
content: How can we be sure that the domicile authorities will accept a not-for-profit organization as small as this?

author: No author specified
content: The proposal is a minimal, sufficient, practical, and simple means to a minimal enough end to meet the regulatory requirements in the domicile. Of course, our proposal will comply with the authorities and not-for-profit foundation’s requirements.

author: No author specified
content: How did the design relate to the European/Swiss group?

author: No author specified
content: The foundation’s design is aligned with and partly inspired by the Swiss initiatives, for which we are very thankful!A Swiss foundation, as proposed by the group’s initiator in mid-2024, may work well for the CH cases. However, it needs to be expanded.

author: No author specified
content: Not thoroughly combining the design with the Swiss group may result in a more difficult start in terms of funding and the loss of a currently unknown number of supporters.

author: No author specified
content: Still, we need a more practical, broader, long-term approach that keeps the KERI inventor’s vision intact. Any local group can have more urgent plans and a directed agenda.

author: No author specified
content: We’ve foreseen a direct funding method within the foundation to accommodate the needs of the Swiss user group and businesses.

author: No author specified
content: How will the KERI foundation eventually become independent of individuals?

author: No author specified
content: We invented a role-based organizational chart. So, yes, the foundation is already independent of individuals, except that the BDFL role is reserved for Dr. Samuel M. Smith.

author: No author specified
content: Trying to become independent of the original inventor too early would be a significant error, especially in the KERI case and because of our stage. Not to mention the apparent lack of respect for the work done, which oozes from an approach to try and outnumber the critical roles with voting power, this setup has failed before (and will again), not only in KERI’s history but in numerous other open-source projects.

author: No author specified
content: If sponsors were dropping out, would you let this happen?

author: No author specified
content: We would do everything possible to bind sponsors to us within the objectives and design of the foundation.

author: No author specified
content: Who are the sponsors who are dropping out?

author: No author specified
content: Please provide the name and the lost opportunity in US $.

author: No author specified
content: Is the chairman practically running the foundation on his own?

author: No author specified
content: One individual has to take up various roles at the beginning of the foundation. In this perspective, anno 2025, the answer to the question is ‘to a great extent, yes’

content: But bear in mind: We developed a role-based organizational chart, an Operational Agreement, and a Memorandum of Understanding. So, it’s a consensus-critical manager-managed operation in the foundation. Without painful consequences, no single role within the foundation could dictate or veto the operation. All competence profiles have been specified and published.

author: No author specified
content: Moreover, the foundation is independent of individuals, except that the BDFL role in the KERI foundation is reserved on a personal basis for Dr. Samuel Smith.

author: No author specified
content: We honestly can’t take people seriously when they complain about a compact KERI foundation, which the original inventor is totally in sync with, and a two-way commitment between the foundation and founder has been established.

author: No author specified
content: This the “Sam Foundation”?!

author: No author specified
content: The chairman and Sam could decide independently of anybody else. To gain something, you also have to give. It would not find the support of a larger community.

author: No author specified
content: It’s the community foundation supported by KERI’s inventor Dr. Samuel M. Smith. Any qualified majority in the board makes decisions on all but a few topics. These topics are reserved for the technical manager with a few veto rights for the original inventor, which conforms to the well-established BDFL model.Having stated this, we will always strive for consensus, but by all means, avoid takeovers or stalemates.

author: No author specified
content: Thus, we wish to protect the original inventor’s work and future role. Furthermore, we wish to gradually develop the Foundation into a business-like, not-for-profit, open-source foundation where takeovers by narrow interest groups are impossible.

author: No author specified
content: We can do this via:A. Statutes and organizational regulationsB. Board composition

author: No author specified
content: Ad A.

author: No author specified
content: Statutes tend to be rigid. We create them, put them in a drawer, respect them, and only take them out when something happens. We will flexibly adjust the organization along the lines we lay out. We’ve used a standard Utah template, “Muli-Member L3C, Manager-Managed Organization Agreement”, for that.And that’s where anyone donating and participating in the roles, tasks, and deliverables laid out has to trust what we do for the community.

author: No author specified
content: Ad B.See the organizational chart, the competence profiles, and the Memorandum of Understanding (Start 2025: status concept)

author: No author specified
content: Who do you trust more to guide this process: the KERI team, including the original inventor, or some local interest group with an urgent agenda?

author: No author specified
content: We aim for the more considerable support that we expect to be given to the proof of work team, the original KERI team.

author: No author specified
content: History

author: No author specified
content: Initially, the idea was to give all parties a voice and bring them together in one forum: the Advisory Board. The founders have reserved a seat on the board with decision-making authority for the leader of that Advisory Board.

author: No author specified
content: This concession isn’t necessary for the current state of affairs, nor would it be the first choice to expand the board, but the founders expressed their appreciation by bundling advice in this board vote.It constitutes the Best of Both Worlds: A small team can act fast and finish the work (5Ws). All stakeholders get a voice, and based on consensus in the advisory board, their chairman/leader has a seat with voting rights on the board of directors to represent the common interests of the stakeholders without slowing down the operations through endless discussions and individual interests.

author: No author specified
content: What is a BDFL and who is it?

author: No author specified
content: A BDFL, which stands for “Benevolent Dictator For Life,” is an informal title given to influential leaders in open-source software development projects. This term typically refers to project founders or primary creators who retain final decision-making authority over the project’s direction and development.The concept of BDFL originated in 1995 and is closely associated with the Python programming language community. Guido van Rossum, the creator of Python, was jokingly named the “First Interim BDFL”.Key characteristics of a BDFL include:1. Ultimate authority on technical decisions2. Influence over coding standards and core design philosophies3. Veto power over proposed changes4. Collaborative work with the community while maintaining a consistent visionNotable examples of BDFLs include:• Guido van Rossum (Python)• Linus Torvalds (Linux kernel)• Larry Wall (Perl)• Yukihiro Matsumoto (Ruby) • Samuel Smith (Keri)It’s important to note that the BDFL title is honorary and based on community trust. If a BDFL were to act irresponsibly, the project could be forked.

author: No author specified
content: What is the decision-making power of the advisory board?

author: No author specified
content: The board selects the voting chair from the advisory board on the main board.

author: No author specified
content: What is the domicile of the foundation?

author: No author specified
content: The domicile is the state of Utah, US. History

author: No author specified
content: A short list of options for domicile stemming from selection criteria and design principles existed. The design process resulted in a uniform request-for-proposal layout, and local service providers’ responses were considered.

author: No author specified
content: Links:Design principlesDomicile selection criteriaWe looked for a domicile that could grow with what the KERI foundation should become in the long run and with a broad scope.

author: No author specified
content: What is the use of a pro-bono advisory board?

author: No author specified
content: To align ideas and input ideas. One individual, the leader of that advisory board, has decision-making power.

author: No author specified
content: For those who strongly advise against the concept of an advisory board

author: No author specified
content: We would reverse the question:What if the KERI plan and your concept are incompatible with Sam’s wish? How important is it to you to operate as a unit?
firstHeadingBeforeElement: For those who strongly advise against the concept of an advisory board

author: No author specified
content: The question is whether we need an advisory board with final decision-making power over the foundation at this stage. For us, it is a clear ‘no’ to that question. Despite that, we have foreseen the appointment of one person from that advisory board with decision-making power.
firstHeadingBeforeElement: For those who strongly advise against the concept of an advisory board

author: No author specified
content: The advisory board we envision has decision-making power via its chairman having a seat with voting rights on the board of directors. Based on consensus, they should get on the advisory board first instead of slowing down decision-making and operations by having a (growing) bunch of people with individual interests and agendas that consume valuable time. It doesn’t add value or speed up the delivery of the 5Ws at this stage.
firstHeadingBeforeElement: For those who strongly advise against the concept of an advisory board

author: No author specified
content: What shaped the communication about the foundation?

author: No author specified
content: Some people didn’t like the way the KERI team group took over the initiative to design a KERI foundation.

author: No author specified
content: The feeling of being surpassed without being heard is not a nice one. The European group may not have noticed that donation-based funding has been on the agenda of the KERI team for quite a while. What follows is a historical perspective on the communication.

author: No author specified
content: Blockchainbird made its first donation to KERI development via GitHub in 2020. The KERI team wasn’t involved in the Swiss initiative, so we tried to unify both initiatives when we heard of it. We’re happy that this effort was received well.

author: No author specified
content: When the first meeting was planned just after DICE 2024 by a European group that did not frequent the weekly KERI Zoom meetings of WebofTrust or TrustoverIP, the inventor of KERI clearly stated at this first meeting that the initiative to set up a KERI foundation is for those who put real money on the table.

author: No author specified
content: You could argue about the talents and abilities needed to get KERI adopted by large companies and organizations, but here’s a significant fact:KERI’s tooling hasn’t matured yet (status 2024), so selling it to larger companies makes no sense. The implementation will diverge from the reference implementation, which still needs to be completed, and the customers will abandon it with an incompatible codebase that must be maintained in an isolated manner.

author: No author specified
content: We consider KERI’s inventor a hard-working multi-talent and should be supported in what he thinks it takes to keep KERI healthy and what the next steps in this process are: open-source foundations set up with the inventor’s blessing have been far more successful in the past than those that bypassed the inventor.

author: No author specified
content: To confirm, the feeling of being surpassed without being heard is unfortunate. As this elaborate answer explains, we believe the experience is mutual. To work together, the KERI team has taken the lead because Blockchainbird (the donation vehicle of Henk van Cann) committed to initially donating 100K US$ to set up a foundation with the design principles mentioned here.

author: No author specified
content: Why do you need an executive team of managers?

author: No author specified
content: The executive team works on operational matters within the envisioned foundation framework. All the roles that are needed are included (anyone can add them if something is missing). Those roles will soon be assigned to functions/persons depending on the phase/scale of the foundation.

author: No author specified
content: Members versus Managers

author: No author specified
content: We think an essential function of the foundation should be to focus on technology, vision, and broad adoption. KERI founder Sam Smith should be there. We have members of the legal entity, the L3C, and to avoid misunderstanding, we use managers for the foundation, which we run in a professional way but not-for-profit.

author: No author specified
content: Why not create a broad board of directors who sufficiently represent the interests of donors?

author: No author specified
content: Our version of the foundation won’t give way to politicians and talkers, and no one will be able to buy influence. It’ll be a “proof of work” organization, not a proof of stake powerhouse.

author: No author specified
content: The interests of donors are represented in other ways:

author: No author specified
content: coopetive design

author: No author specified
content: advisory board with one voting chair on the board

author: No author specified
content: directly funded projects

author: No author specified
content: In this stage, we can’t allow roles representing narrower interests to outnumber the votes of the critical roles on the board.

author: No author specified
content: You have to let go of control. How are you going to do this?

author: No author specified
content: Originally, this idea was solely to give all parties a voice and bring that together in one forum. The proposed managers of the designed board happily reserve a seat on the board for this purpose, with decision-making authority: the leader of the Advisory Board.

author: No author specified
content: If anyone asks to let go of control, have you put your money where your mouth is?

author: No author specified
content: The founding chairman has committed to donating to get the foundation started. So far, we haven’t seen anyone else making a similar commitment.

author: No author specified
content: So, he wants to set up this vehicle to ensure that the internal processes are efficient, funds are spent effectively, and the required targets are met.  Van Cann: “I’ll stop when I think: would I leave this system alone and trust its inner workings? And only when the answer is ‘yes’”

author: No author specified
content: Your proposal is very complex. Isn’t this concept difficult to “sell?”

author: No author specified
content: We’re keeping the leadership team small. Moreover, we don’t think that we make it complex. On the contrary, we avoid organizational complexity and focus on accelerating the urgently required development processes.

author: No author specified
content: With the confirmed support for this setup by KERI’s inventor, we are ready to go and can focus on finishing the job (5Ws) starting today.

author: No author specified
content: Please, do not to mix complexity with elaboration in details. It’s true we have a thoughtful and comprehensive setup.

author: No author specified
content: 2. Practical setup of the foundation			 (1)

author: No author specified
content: Bitcoin has Bitcoin Improvement Proposals. Why doesn’t KERI have a similar mechanism?

author: No author specified
content: KIPs – KERI Improvement Protocols so that there will also be space for market-driven instead of only technically driven.

author: No author specified
content: Good proposal; we’ll extend the current KIDS in this direction.

author: No author specified
content: 3. Projects			 (7)

author: No author specified
content: Can I withdraw my sponsorship/donation?

author: No author specified
content: After a careful exchange of terms and the donor contract, there is the usual cooling-off period to sign the contract.However, as soon as the contract is signed, the donor is obligated to pay the donorship, and we will have to strictly enforce that. A donor can only lay off the obligation by having another donor replace him/her.This strict debtor management is necessary because we must deliver on our promises and need the finances to do so.

author: No author specified
content: Having presented the general rule, individual cases can be discussed with the chairman of the Foundation to see if there is a way out.

author: No author specified
content: How can I offer one of my developers to help code KERI?

author: No author specified
content: Look for existing projects on our website or submit your proposal. Within a foundation project, roles will be selected for assignment, candidates will be assessed, and officers will be appointed and paid for their deliverables.

author: No author specified
content: Example calculation

author: No author specified
content: Suppose you suggest that Developer Y code with us. Great idea and a lovely proposal!Suppose we agree on Y’s suitability for any vacant project roles (it could be your own freshly established KERI foundation project).

author: No author specified
content: In that case, we hire Y for the same amount that Y’s gross salary involves.

author: No author specified
content: The extra step is needed to keep the foundation independent of individuals and their bosses.

author: No author specified
content: How do we pay attention to urgent matters in country X?

author: No author specified
content: The KERI Foundation has a long-term and global scope. However, can direct part of the team and its agenda to a local scope, directly funded by interest groups for this particular local scope.

author: No author specified
content: Why are there veto rights for the foundation’s chairman and the technical manager?

author: No author specified
content: In terms of execution, they are the only roles you can currently translate operationally into functions. See the other parts of the website to dive deep into their competence profiles that lay out their task, responsibilities, and rights as foundation managers.

author: No author specified
content: Besides the managers of the Foundation, whose rights are laid out in the Operational Agreement (Feb 2025: status concept) and the Memorandum of Understanding (Feb 2025: status concept), we have the members of the steering L3C.There are no veto rights for the members of the L3C should there be a draw in the votes of the L3C, and Sam Smith is still a member of the L3C; he gets the privilege to add one extra member vote to get out of the stalemate.

author: No author specified
content: Why did the KERI Foundation (KF) decide to maintain separate code repositories instead of contributing directly to existing Web of Trust (WoT) repositories?

author: No author specified
content: The KERI Foundation decided to maintain its own repositories to focus more on further maturing the KERISuite and to establish a governance structure that is clearer and more streamlined.

author: No author specified
content: While the WoT community remains volunteer-driven, KF aims to manage directed funds and funded projects more efficiently. This setup allows KF to work on targeted developments while ensuring the code remains open and licensed under Apache 2.0. The decision was not made to split the community but to provide a more structured way to advance the KERISuite.

author: No author specified
content: Why have separate KERI Foundation (KF) repositories alongside existing Web of Trust (WoT) repositories?

author: No author specified
content: The primary concern is that newcomers may find it confusing to understand the relationship between the different repositories and their purposes.

author: No author specified
content: Historically, there have been multiple overlapping initiatives like ToIP, IETF, WoT, and DIF, which already caused confusion. Adding KF repositories could further complicate things for those unfamiliar with the ecosystem.  Newcomers might ask, “What lives where?” and feel that the community is fragmented. Ensuring clarity through proper documentation and syncing practices could help address this issue.

author: No author specified
content: The main reason for having separate KF repositories is that the open-source code that belongs there will have an enhanced balanced legal framework and decision-making unit.

author: No author specified
content: Why is the foundation concerned with other programming languages of KERI implementations?

author: No author specified
content: Security and reputation are key here.

author: No author specified
content: A responsible tech team should manage this auditing/steering under Technical Managers’ leadership.

author: No author specified
content: Compliance and API compatibility with the reference installation should be the deciding factors. More of those key criteria could be noted in the organizational regulations.

author: No author specified
content: There will be a tech team managing all technical and security issues. Under the Technical Managers’ leadership, but not on a day-to-day basis. This role will be gatekeeping to ensure all operations are aligned with the strategic objectives and vision.Technical Management of the KERI Foundation is appointed to Sam Smith as long as he wishes to perform this role and is capable of doing so. Because of his special background as the KERISuite inventor and co-founder of the KF, implementations should be in full width and non-negotiably vetoable by him for as long as he’s in a Technical Management position.Apart from him, no other individual in this future position will have a veto right.

author: No author specified
content: 4. Licenses, IP, and standardization			 (2)

author: No author specified
content: Shouldn’t Licensing and IP be put in the statutes so that no Board, in the future, could change that decision?

author: No author specified
content: The inventor has well-educated opinions on licensing an IP and is very well-suited to assess future changes and adapt to them for the well-being of the open-source KERI Suite.

author: No author specified
content: That is why his role needs to be able to veto changes. In the future, when Samuel Smith steps back for any reason, we could adjust the statutes so that no board can change that decision in the future.

author: No author specified
content: Why not go straight for an ISO-TC proposal to get KERI, ACDC, and CESR standardized as swiftly as possible?

author: No author specified
content: It could be a parallel approach to our effort via the ToIP/Linux foundation. However, it takes a lot of work and time to pursue ISO-TC individually.

author: No author specified
content: Our take is to go through a recognized body like the Linux Foundation. Because ISO does not allow them to go straight to them.

author: No author specified
content: 5. Funding			 (5)

author: No author specified
content: Can I withdraw my sponsorship/donation?

author: No author specified
content: After a careful exchange of terms and the donor contract, there is the usual cooling-off period to sign the contract.However, as soon as the contract is signed, the donor is obligated to pay the donorship, and we will have to strictly enforce that. A donor can only lay off the obligation by having another donor replace him/her.This strict debtor management is necessary because we must deliver on our promises and need the finances to do so.

author: No author specified
content: Having presented the general rule, individual cases can be discussed with the chairman of the Foundation to see if there is a way out.

author: No author specified
content: How are you going to attract sponsors and donors?

author: No author specified
content: We envision frain-grained methods to onboard international sponsors and donors to (directly funded) projects under supervision and value-adding by the foundation.

author: No author specified
content: That doesn’t mean we’re perfect. Supposing you have a better proposal in mind, these are the questions that we pose ourselves in a local context but bearing the greater picture in mind:

author: No author specified
content: Who will step on board and donate? How much? Probability?

author: No author specified
content: How do I envision the strategy and bringing in funding partners outside of my local scope? We need those because the geographical scope needs to be international.

author: No author specified
content: What contribution do my strategy and my group make to the broader foundation goal?

author: No author specified
content: What would be my forecast for raising funds in the next 2 to 3 years? Targets/industries and anticipated amounts? What conditions would I offer and agree upon?

author: No author specified
content: How do I value the global objectives compared to the local scope?

author: No author specified
content: What share of the total required funding (5Ws phase) do I estimate that my local jurisdiction will put on the table?

author: No author specified
content: How would I propose to widen the geographical scope and attract international partners?

author: No author specified
content: I want to donate, but I’d like to know upfront what will you do with my money?

author: No author specified
content: Thank you for considering donating. This is how we onboard donors:

author: No author specified
content: We formulate portfolios in which money will flow

author: No author specified
content: We accept donors who want to sign a donor contract matching the portfolio

author: No author specified
content: An obligation to pay the donorship money is established via a donor contract

author: No author specified
content: Look at the projects currently at hand and the vacant positions we’re filling in on this site!

author: No author specified
content: We’re ready to accept donors from March 2025. Because we can’t make a deal, we can talk to donors in a non-binding way.

author: No author specified
content: Is there a sales tiger or a fundraiser needed to close the deals of funding?

author: No author specified
content: We have quite a few people on Zoom calls who have these capabilities.

author: No author specified
content: Yes, we’ve described the fundraiser in a competence profile, so we’ll get there to install an officer for this task and consult the current team of participants when the position is vacant.

author: No author specified
content: The status of early 2025 is that the L3C + Operational Agreement + MoU legal structure was set up by the end of 2024.Foremost, we need the organization, the donor contract, and the bank account because otherwise, any deal can’t be closed. Of course, enthusiasts can always start the preparatory/exploratory work.

author: No author specified
content: Our plan is to get the funding rolling, immediately start the development of the 5Ws with this, and raise more funds to support developers who completed the 5Ws. Only then will KERI adoption be sustainable. A sales tiger selling projects too early invokes a large user base too-early, and this might backfire.

author: No author specified
content: What about money flowing to another foundation that supports KERI?

author: No author specified
content: We have to keep the community together as well as we can. But not at all cost. How important do you think it is to operate as a unit?

author: No author specified
content: Anyone would prefer to be “Sam Smith Certified,” and to get there; any donor has to establish a connection with the one and only KERI foundation supported by the inventor.

author: No author specified
content: We consider any other foundation that claims to be the KERI foundation a business. We will happily and in a friendly way offer services to them and could coopetively move forward.

author: No author specified
content: 7. Decision making			 (3)

author: No author specified
content: Are people willing to donate money and development power if they have no say in the outcome?

author: No author specified
content: Donating money and donating time are two distinct things. Money donors have a say in the outcome.

author: No author specified
content: Our foundation offers fine-grained benefits and influence models through donations. The website has information on direct funding, advisory boards, marketing, and events. You have a tiered say from tiered donations.

author: No author specified
content: The KERI Foundation won’t be able to accept donations in kind directly. The process will be as follows: roles will be selected for assignment, candidates will be assessed, and officers will be appointed and paid for their deliverables.We do not want a dependency on individual contributors with a greater reach than their roles, tasks, responsibilities, and deliverables.

author: No author specified
content: Aren’t there more shoulders needed than three board managers to drive KERI adoption?

author: No author specified
content: At the start of 2025, we only have two!  We’re engaging parties to bring in the leader of the Advisory Board and one of the board managers. But we agree that we need more shoulders, eventually. First, we want to start the donation flow and complete the 5Ws to meet the adoption expectations.

author: No author specified
content: Phase 2 adds more shoulders to the board. It can be prepared in a (directly funded) working group in Phase 1. Feel free to take an advance on this point.We’ve heard of a four-manager board up to the sixteen-managers board. All good to consider in Phase 1, but we postpone establishing any solution of more than three right after delivering on Phase 1, the 5Ws.

author: No author specified
content: The reason is that we want to free the crucial development from endless discussions and decision-making.

author: No author specified
content: Why not put all rules in the statutes?

author: No author specified
content: So authorities can audit, and the board has to decide on changes?

author: No author specified
content: A Utah-registered L3C, a not-for-profit organization, steers the KERI Foundation.

author: No author specified
content: Processing everything into articles of association is very static. You have to make dynamic decisions based on constantly changing insights. You want to keep adjusting.

author: No author specified
content: We have two principal guidelines in this perspective:

author: No author specified
content: Adjust as standardized as possible in the Operational Agreement, which is based on a template Muli-member L3C, Manager-Managed from the state of Utah,

author: No author specified
content: Put everything else in the Memorandum of Understanding plus the Competence Profile of the key management positions in the foundation. These latter documents are an integral part of – and bi-directionally referred to and from 1, the Operational Agreement.

author: No author specified
content: 8. Board composition			 (2)

author: No author specified
content: Doesn’t your board look like a management team too much?

author: No author specified
content: I wonder whether a 3-person board of directors is so functional and effective.The foundation design is a cathedral (from The Cathedral and The Bazaar, by Eric S. Raymond) with just three people on the board.

author: No author specified
content: Yes, it is a team of managers (because we’ve reserved the term members for the steering L3C). However, the cathedral is a mischaracterization. In history, there have been numerous successful foundations to support the development and implementation of open-source software that started with a three-person board of directors, including the inventor/benevolent dictator:

author: No author specified
content: The Free Software Foundation (FSF). When it was founded by Richard Stallman in 1985, it had a board of directors with three members.

author: No author specified
content: The Open Source Initiative (OSI). The OSI was founded in 1998 by Eric S. Raymond and Bruce Perens, along with a third initial member, to promote and protect open-source software by certifying licenses and advocating for open-source principles. While the exact composition of the board has evolved, the foundation initially had a small core group of influential members guiding its mission.

author: No author specified
content: Apache Software Foundation (ASF): Founded in 1999, the ASF initially had a small group of individuals instrumental in its creation. The initial board included several key contributors to the Apache HTTP Server project, though the specific number of members can vary over time.

author: No author specified
content: Python Software Foundation (PSF): The PSF started with a small board in 2001 to support the Python programming language. The initial board of directors was composed of a few key figures in the Python community, including Guido van Rossum, the creator of Python.

author: No author specified
content: Linux Foundation: Although the Linux Foundation (founded in 2000 as the Open Source Development Labs) had a larger initial advisory board, its governing structure initially included a small, focused group of influential members from the open-source community, including Linus Torvalds, who played a significant role in guiding the foundation’s early direction.

author: No author specified
content: Shouldn’t it be a broad board instead of a management team?

author: No author specified
content: I believe the structure is too narrow. We need more Board members.

author: No author specified
content: One of our design principles is that we run it like a company, but it IS a board in a legal sense.

author: No author specified
content: It is only reasonable, safe, and logical to first finish things in a small technical team and only then start diversifying.

author: No author specified
content: We’ll reverse the question: How do you balance board managers over interest groups and continents? Does it become a political mess quickly?

author: No author specified
content: NSA’s Five Eyes Cyber Security Agency has issued an urgent warning about AI and cybersecurity.

author: No author specified
content: But we believe it raises an even bigger question:

author: No author specified
content: How do we secure society without sacrificing digital freedom?

author: No author specified
content: Cybersecurity and digital identity expert Dr. Samuel M. Smith, inventor of KERI, explains why the answer may fundamentally reshape the future of identity online and human freedom.

author: No author specified
content: The questions Dr. Samuel M. Smith answers in the video:

author: No author specified
content: 1. AI and Human Authority

author: No author specified
content: The Five Eyes statement calls for AI-assisted defense. How do we ensure AI remains subordinate to humans, law, policy, and cryptographically verifiable identity infrastructure rather than becoming a de facto authority?

author: No author specified
content: Follow-up: What are the hardest legal, technical, or governance mechanisms that are needed to ensure every significant AI action or AI result remains attributable to accountable humans and organizations?Go to AI and Human Authority on YouTube

author: No author specified
content: 2. Identity in the AI Era

author: No author specified
content: The statement calls for stronger identity and access controls. Why are today’s identity systems insufficient against AI-powered threats, and what specifically does KERISuite change?

author: No author specified
content: Follow-up: Which weaknesses in current identity systems become most dangerous when attackers can operate at AI speed and scale?

author: No author specified
content: 3. Cyber Resilience After Breach

author: No author specified
content: The statement assumes breaches are inevitable. In a KERI-based architecture, what remains trustworthy after servers, databases, cloud providers, or certificate authorities are compromised?

author: No author specified
content: Follow-up: How does KERISuite shift security from protecting infrastructure to protecting secrets that reveal verifiable truth and provenance?

author: No author specified
content: Go to Cyber Resilience After Breach on YouTube

author: No author specified
content: 4. Privacy Without Surveillance

author: No author specified
content: Can strong security, accountability, and resilience be achieved without continuous monitoring and surveillance? How does KERISuite balance privacy with accountability?

author: No author specified
content: Follow-up: What is the minimum amount of data disclosure needed to achieve accountability without creating a surveillance society?Go to Privacy Without Surveillance on YouTube

author: No author specified
content: 5. The Stark Choice

author: No author specified
content: If frontier AI reaches superhuman offensive cyber capability, do our choices become increasingly stark?

author: No author specified
content: A. Total surveillance, continuous monitoring, and zero-privacy identity systems.

author: No author specified
content: B. KERISuite, where trust comes from cryptographically verifiable identity based on fault-tolerant key management, provenance, and accountability.

author: No author specified
content: Why do you believe there may be no stable middle ground between these two futures?

author: No author specified
content: Follow-up: As AI capabilities increase, why does continuous-tracking or surveillance-based trust become less sustainable than cryptographically verifiable trust?Go to The Stark Choice on YouTube

author: No author specified
content: 6. Keeping Humans Digitally Free

author: No author specified
content: As AI becomes the dominant interface to the digital world, how must SEDI/KERISuite be applied to AI itself so that people remain digitally free rather than becoming exploitable, surveilled, tracked, manipulated, or controlled?

author: No author specified
content: Should AI be trusted to protect human freedom, or should AI itself be constrained by a cryptographically verifiable trust architecture that keeps ultimate authority with people?

author: No author specified
content: Follow-up: What would a world look like where every AI agent operates under cryptographically verifiable authority by delegation and thus accountability?

author: No author specified
content: 7. The Race Against Time

author: No author specified
content: If frontier AI is advancing on a timescale of months rather than years, how quickly can society realistically deploy either of these futures at a global scale?

author: No author specified
content: A. Continuous identity, monitoring, and surveillance infrastructure capable of tracking humans, organizations, devices, and AI agents.

author: No author specified
content: B. SEDI/KERISuite-based infrastructure built on cryptographically verifiable identity, fault-tolerant key management, provenance, and accountability.

author: No author specified
content: Which approach can be deployed more quickly, more safely, and more humanely at a planetary scale?

author: No author specified
content: Follow-up: What are the biggest technical, economic, political, and adoption barriers to deploying SEDI/KERISuite fast enough to become a viable alternative before surveillance-based systems become entrenched?Go to The Race Against Time; Keeping Humans Digitally Free on YouTube

author: No author specified
content: 8. Conclusion

author: No author specified
content: SEDI, the last best hope against AI.

author: No author specified
content: Go to State Endorsed Digital Identity on YouTube
